data.title: Senior Plc: Why I Remain Bullish Despite Challenges Faced On Airplane Production
data.description:
Senior plc (OTCPK:SNIRF, LON: SNR) is one of the companies that I believe can benefit from longer-term increases in commercial airplane production and capitalize on growing demand in the defense and energy markets. My buy rating from February this year has so far yielded a 4.3% return compared to 4.8% decline for the S&P 500 (SP500), but we do note that the listing in London lost nearly 7%. Denominated in dollars, the stock price has not move at all.

In this report, I discuss the most recent earnings and assess whether the positives I see on the civil aerospace market and defense markets are enough to maintain my buy rating.

The section below has been includes parts that I previously discussed, but readers are urged to read the section as it has been expanded and updated with the most recent figures.

Senior plc Has Attractive End Market Exposure

Senior pls reports its results through two segments, namely the Aerospace division which and Flexonics. The aerospace division includes civil aerospace, defense and related markets while Flexonics includes sales of thermal management and fluid conveyance solutions for industries such as automotive, energy and medical.

The aerospace segment accounts for 68% of the sales with civil aerospace accounting for 46% of the sales, 13% is derived from defense and 9% is derived from related markets. The remaining 32% of sales is derived in the Flexonics segment with 13% from Power & Energy and 19% from Land Vehicles. We see that year-on-year, the share in sales dropped in all end markets except for civil aerospace and defense. This underscores the growth momentum for aerospace and defense.

Within the civil business, we see that 70% of the sales is generated from single aisle airplanes such as the Boeing 737 MAX and Airbus A320neo as well as regional and business jets. The remaining 30% is generated from the widebody airplane market.

While commercial airplanes manufacturing is dominated by Boeing (BA) and Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF), we see that generally no customer in the mix accounts for more than 8% in the total sales mix. This is driven by the fact that the company does not only deliver directly to Boeing and Airbus, but also serves as a supplier for the aero engine manufacturers and has exposure to defense contractors as well as industrial customers. If we look at where the commercial airplane products end up we see that 66% of the sales can be attributed to Airbus airplanes and the remaining 34% goes to Airbus.

As I previously discussed, there is a lot of upside on commercial airplane production that should be beneficial to Senior:

  • Boeing is set to reach a production rate of 38 Boeing 737 MAX airplanes a month in mid-2025 and the company provided a strong plan to get there in a way that allows for sustainable production growth.
  • While Airbus is no longer putting a timeline on hitting a production rate of 75 airplanes a month, it remains the goal to get there. Deliveries on the program were up 5% in 2024 hitting an equivalent rate of 50 airplanes per month and I believe the equivalent delivery rate could be 10% higher in 2025.
  • The Airbus A220 program is ramping up with deliveries up 10% in 2024 and the longer-term target is to reach a production rate of 14 airplanes a month, indicating the potential for production to more than double from current levels.
  • The Boeing 787 program temporarily reduced production to below five in 2025, but this has snapped back to five airplanes a month and a step-up in production is expected this year, with the rate ramping up to 10 a month by 2026.
  • Airbus A350 deliveries declined by 7 units in 2024 equivalent to a rate of around five per month with plans to ramp production to 12 per month by 2028.

On the key programs, we see that there is a very positive outlook. There always has been a positive outlook for higher production rates, but there also have been disruptions to the ramp. Important, however, is the fact that the longer-term picture remains intact which means that pressures seen now are more near-term pressures than actual long-term pressures, and we have seen that Boeing was more positive on the supply chain health for turbofans and fuselages during its recent earnings calls. Encouragingly, we are seeing a lot of positive momentum building. Execution risk remains, but to me, it seems that many suppliers are better positioned to support rate increases than they were a year ago.

A possible risk is that there is exposure to the automotive industry and that is a market that has been quite soft and recent trade turmoil is not going to help revitalizing that industry any time soon.

Soft Sales Growth In FY2024 For Senior

While I am upbeat on the prospects for Senior, the FY24 revenues did not show significant growth with growth of only 1%. I mainly attribute this to continued challenges that Boeing and Airbus have faced in 2024 and the strike at Boeing of course also provided a headwind. Adjusted profits increased 2% to £46.5 million indicating margins that were stable at 4.8% while free cash flow increased to £17.3 million.

Aerospace revenues climbed 9.9% to £660.8 million with margins expanding form 4.4% to 4.6% for 14.3% increase in adjusted operating profit to £30.4 . This growth was partially offset by Flexonics where sales declined nearly 6% to £317.7 million and adjusted operating income declined 3% to £35.1 million.

So, we see that the company has relatively low margins which is not quite appreciable but on higher airplane production rates I do believe these margins should increase significantly. At the same time, we do note that nearly 60% of the Flexonics sales are related to the automotive industry and that is not an industry that is in a favorable position at this point in time. That means that there is a risk that aerospace growth will be partially offset by weakness in the Flexonics business in the worst case scenario.

Senior plc Stock Is Still A Buy But With A Marginally Lower Price Target

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Note: The price targets are based on the listing at the London Stock Exchange, which provides the more liquid (and thus preferred) investment opportunity. Currencies have been converted to USD.

For Senior plc, the EBITDA in 2024 was 11.4% ahead of expectations including a 6.7 points driven by currency effects. For 2025 and 2026, EBITDA estimates have been increased by roughly 6% for an annual growth rate of 19% in EBITDA through 2027.

Free cash flow came in above expectations, but that seems to be largely driven by a pull forward on cash receipts has been decreased by roughly the $7 million beat in 2024. Nonetheless, we are expecting a significant improvement in free cash flow as airplane production rates increase providing higher volumes and higher margins to the business.

As a result, I am maintaining my buy rating for Senior plc with a $2.75 price target which is modestly lower compared to the $2.84 price target as debt is expected to be higher. Against the OTC listing, the upside would be 34% and 36% against the listing at the London Stock Exchange.

Conclusion: Senior plc Has Attractive Growth Drivers

There is no denying in the fact that there is some pressure in the Flexonics business and commercial airplane production rate increases have been more slow paced than initially expected. That has been a risk in 2024 and continues to be a risk, but I do believe that jet makers now are better positioned to increase airplane production. This should be beneficial to the company’s topline and provide positive volume effects on margins leading to profit growth exceeding top line growth. As a result, I am maintaining my bullish view on Senior plc.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PepsiCo: Stable And Incredibly Cheap Despite The Risks
data.description:
PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP) fell to $130 last week, which is its lowest level since 2021. The EPS announcement in the last quarter was below expectations. Tariffs and the decline in sales volumes in North America contributed significantly to this decline. But I think this can be corrected quite well in the short term and the company can return to its high-performance days. I will try to explain to you why I think so and why I am still on the “buy” side, using data and analysis.

Financials

The company reported a 1.81% YoY and 35.51% QoQ decline in revenue in the last quarter. While this may seem negative on its own, it is important to look at the bigger picture. Because since 2022 there is a clear seasonality and at least the QoQ decline is quite normal.

Since 2022 to 2025, PEP reported an average 35.52% QoQ decline in revenue in the first quarter of every year. The revenue in the first quarter of 2025 was announced with a decline of exactly this average level, 35.49%. But it is important to note the following. Until 2025, PEP reported positive YoY revenue growth on average of 6.47% in the first quarter of every year since 2017. But 2025 is the first year that there was a decline YoY since 2017. Undoubtedly, this decrease in revenue has a large share in the stock's decline.

The company management said that this decrease was due to the macroeconomic environment becoming increasingly dynamic and complex, including new tariffs and low demand in many markets. Demand weakened in North America, especially in the ready-to-eat segment where global volumes fell by 3%. The company also faced negative supply chain costs.

Not only that, PEP’s organic revenue (excluding the effects of currency fluctuations, acquisitions and divestitures) was actually up 1.2% in the quarter. But reported (GAAP) revenue fell due to the significant negative impact of foreign exchange rates. Currency differences reduced net revenue by 3%. In constant currency terms, PEP’s core business did not actually decline but the weakening of the U.S. dollar against other currencies reduced the value of international sales.

So what are my Q2 2025 revenue expectations? First, I want to compare the company’s guidance for Q4 2024 to its guidance for Q1 2025, so I can understand how much the company’s own guidance changed in the quarter.

There is no change in the company's organic revenue growth, which is a good sign. In terms of core constant currency EPS, the company expected mid-single digit growth in the last quarter of 2024. It has now lowered this expectation and stated that it no longer expects growth. While the company expected an increase in the reported core EPS, it now expects a 3% decline due to currency movements. As a result of that, the company is on the more cautious side right now. So I will also take this cautious stance into account when calculating my expectations.

I said that there is a clear seasonality in PEP's revenue. According to this seasonality, I calculate that every year the 2nd quarter grew by an average of 24.24% from QoQ and an average of 6.02% from YoY. Based on this my revenue expectation for the next quarter is between $22.3-23.8 billion. . But as I said in the previous paragraph, I think that since I am closer to the low side an revenue of around $22.3 billion will be announced. This means YoY 1.06% decrease in the 2nd quarter. So I think that the negativity in foreign exchange will also affect the revenue in this quarter.

I calculate that since 2021, the net margin is 11.01% on average. If there is such a margin in the second quarter, net income will be around $2.45 billion. Based on this, my EPS expectation is around $1.78. As seen on the Seeking Alpha page market expects an EPS of $2.05. So I think that EPS will be announced below expectations in this quarter.

As a result of that, even though the company's financials maintain their stability, I see that they are moving towards a more stagnant side this year. Since there is no structural or operational problem, I think that it is only a temporary negative, even though the macroeconomic changes affect the company's income.

Economic Profitability

Unlike financial profitability, the economic profitability of the company is a method I use to analyze whether the company is growing profitably. WACC and ROIC are the key data when I'm using this method.

When I look at PEP's historical economic profitability since 2021, I see that it oscillates between 20% and 7% since 2022. Even though the positive economic profitability is not in a certain trend, it seems stable. Since I said experienced that stocks with stable economic profitability are in an uptrend in the long term, I see this as very positive for PEP.

Dividend Analysis

We focus on the subject of investment, but in this process I think it is very important to do this analysis for investors who expect regular returns by talking not only about capital but also about the dividends distributed by the stock.

PEP's dividend per share was growing continuously since they started to distribute dividends. In this analysis, I will only use the data since 2014. The dividend growth in the last 5 years is 7.20% and the growth in the last 10 years is 7.52%. When adjusted for inflation, PEP's dividend growth in the last 5 years is 2.58% and the growth in the last 10 years drops to 4.33%. So PEP has positive dividend growth in real terms every year and investors can be sure that they have beaten inflation with peace of mind.

When I take the average of PEP's historical dividend yield since 2014 I get 2.93%. Even though the decline in the share price caused the dividend yield to diverge from this average and increased it to 4.11%, it is important to say that the average is at this level.

I want to look at the free cash flow to examine whether the company have the ability to pay its dividend. In the last 5 years PEP's free cash flow growth rate was at 5.51%. Even though the company's cash flow decreased from 2016 to 2020, it compensated for these losses, especially with a growth of 41.43% in 2023.

Even though we saw a 9.22% decrease in cash flow last year, as can be seen from Seeking Alpha's growth page YoY leveraged FCF growth (FWD) is 12.86% and cash flow per share growth is 10.30%. So it can't be said that cash flow health is bad. Based on this, in my opinion, PEP will not have any difficulty in paying its dividends to its investors.

DCF Analysis

Speaking of cash flow in the dividend section, I think that using the discounted cash flow method is useful in determining the intrinsic value of the stock too.

Year 2024 2023 2022 2021
Free Cash Flow $7,190.00 $7,920.00 $5,600.00 $6,990.00
YoY -9.22% 41.43% -19.89% 9.73%

I said that I determined that the company's average cash flow growth in the last 5 years was 5.51%. But since this is based on old data I think that taking Seeking Alpha's forward growth expectations into account when performing the discounted cash flow analysis will yield much more accurate results.

Year GDP Growth
2024 2.80%
2023 2.90%
2022 2.50%
2021 6.10%
2020 -2.20%
2019 2.60%
2018 3.00%
2017 2.50%
2016 1.80%
2015 2.90%
2014 2.50%
2013 2.10%
2012 2.30%
2011 1.60%
2010 2.70%

In addition to that I calculated the perpetual growth rate as 2.48% by excluding the 2020-2021 period, which was the extraordinary COVID period and taking the average of annual GDP growth since 2010. I obtained PEP's beta as 0.48 and the risk-free rate as 4.36% from the US 10-year bond data. I calculated the SP500's average return over the last 10 years as 12.27% and the equity risk premium as 7.91%.

Total Debt $48,518.00
Market Value of Equity $178,230.00
Net Interest Expense (Annualized) $1,056.00
Cost of Debt 2.18%
Tax Rate 20.00%
After Tax Cost of Debt 1.74%
Debt Weight 21.40%
Equity Weight 78.60%
Risk Free Rate 4.36%
Beta 0.48
SP500 10Y Average Return 12.27%
ERP 7.91%
Cost of Equity 8.16%
WACC 6.78%

I calculated data such as debt weight, equity weight, cost of equity using the data I received from PEP's latest financial report. In this process, I took into account the effective tax rate of 20% as written on page 3 of the earnings report. In light of this data, I have calculated the current most current WACC rate as 6.78%.

Even though I got the FCF growth (FWD) from Seeking Alpha, since I generally calculate 5 years back and 5 years forward, I also calculated the FCF growth 5 years forward and calculated the terminal value for 2030.

Year 2025 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030 Terminal Value
Future Cash Flows $7,930.57 $8,747.42 $9,648.40 $10,642.19 $11,738.33 $12,947.38 $308,050.45
Present Value $7,426.74 $7,671.27 $7,923.85 $8,184.75 $8,454.24 $8,732.61 $194,570.74

In light of these variables DCF analysis for PEP tells me that the intrinsic value of the company is $147.97. So the stock is currently around the price it should be. I think it makes sense to proceed with the conclusions drawn from this analysis while continuing with the other valuation and price performance analyses in the article.

Forward P/E Analysis

When I look at the company's historical forward P/E I must say that I encountered a view that I have rarely seen. Until 2024, I spot that the company's forward P/E was priced around the average of 22.16x. We saw low prices like 18.42x in 2019 or high prices like 25.97x in 2021, but these dramatic divergences only indicated buying and selling opportunities.

In the decline after 2024, the company's forward P/E pierced the lowest ratio it had seen, 18.42x and fell to 14.39x. This was the lowest forward P/E ratio PEP had seen since 2016. The current forward P/E is around 16.74x, which again shows that PEP is at an amazingly cheap price.

While PEP wasn't generally priced lower than the forward P/E of the SP500 index before 2024, it started to be priced cheaper than the SP500 for the first time after 2024 and especially last month the forward P/E difference between them increased to 6.47x. Currently, PEP is priced very cheaply compared to the SP500 index.

When I take the average of the forward P/Es of PEP's competitors MNST, KO and KDP, I reach 25.31x. PEP is currently in a significantly cheaper position than its competitors.

As a result, PEP is in a cheaper position than ever before, both in terms of its history, its competitors and the index it is in. I see this as a great opportunity for PEP investors, as it is not a situation we often encounter.

Price Performance and Charting

PEP’s price performance in the long term is incredible. Since the 1990s, the company was usually been a bullish bet. Even though we saw declines in 1993, 1998, 2002 and 2008, as we saw in 2024, PEP always emerged strong from these declines and continued to rise, and the stock closed 31 of the 40 years since 1985 with a profit and only 9 with a loss. This means that historically, the probability of an investor closing the year with a loss is only 22.50%. Furthermore, if PEP closes the year with a profit, this profit is on average 12.59% which is a tremendous return rate.

I use the Hodrick Prescott filter in this analysis, which I use to determine the trend in every charting analysis. When I analyze the company's 20-month HP filter since 1993, I see that the company negatively diverged from the filter by around 20% in the bullish moves I mentioned at the beginning. Currently, PEP diverged from the filter by around 17%. So this gives me a signal that the bottom was seen and that a slow turn will come. I am returning to the weekly time frame to make this a little more certain.

In the analysis I make in the weekly time frame, in addition to the HP filter, I also receive a lot of support from the price action method and the DCA indicator I developed. The DCA indicator is an indicator that evaluates and determines whether a stock is particularly overbought or oversold by processing various indicators.

PEP was in a downtrend since 2023 and this is clearly seen. And right now, I spot $158 and $130 levels as historical price action support levels. The $130 level was tested as support many times, especially between 2019-2022 and PEP has managed to hold on to it. So I think this level is a really solid support level.

In addition to that, even though there was a downtrend since 2023, it shouldn't be forgotten that there was an uptrend since 2014. This uptrend was tested twice during the 2018 and 2020 COVID downturn. This is the first time since COVID that this trend is being tested again at $130. Since $130 was seen as support many times and the uptrend is now at this level, I think this level is a very good level for PEP to return.

The DCA indicator gives a signal every time PEP enters the oversold region, and it is giving a signal right now, and it shows that PEP is oversold. I had said that the negative divergence from the HP filter reached 17% in the monthly time frame and this divergence also exceeded 17% in the weekly time frame.

Even though the DCF analysis indicates that the intrinsic value of the stock is $147, the fact that the technical indicators are giving a loud oversold signal and the forward P/E is at an all-time low makes it logical for me to stay on the buy side for PEP.

Right now, I think that if PEP stays above $130, it will rise to the 40-week HP filter of $150. I can't speak so ambitiously that PEP can break the $158 resistance for now because the point that DCF is most affected by concerning the intrinsic value is the perpetual growth rate, and since the Trump tariffs are very likely to affect growth this year, I think the stock will continue to be pressured in this direction.

For now, I only see PEP holding at $130, and I think it is a very good buy point.

Risks

The risks facing PEP are divided into two. There are risks affecting both the sector and PEP. First of all, it is a clear fact that food and beverage volumes said decreased in North America. The fact that food sales decreased by 1% and beverage sales decreased by 3% in the last quarter shows that consumers are turning to healthier options and moving away from traditional snacks and sugary drinks.

In addition to that, the tariffs applied to soda concentrate (10%) and the threat of a 25% tariff applied to aluminum continue to increase production costs and put more pressure on margins. It is really difficult to pass on these cost increases to consumers without further negatively affecting demand, especially in price-sensitive categories. PEP has to create a very good strategy in this situation.

Conclusion

PEP is a stock that is continuously increasing and staying in an uptrend in the long term. Right now, it continues to stand tall despite being affected by macroeconomic negativity. In addition to that, its economic profitability is quite stable. Even though DCF analysis indicates that the intrinsic value of the stock is $147 and therefore the stock is very close to its intrinsic value, the fact that the valuations have positioned the stock at a cheaper place provides a great reason for investors to buy. While the dividend health and yield make PEP a very good investment for investors who want regular investments, technical indicators confirm that the stock is cheaper than ever, and it is in an oversold position. I currently think that the stock will hold at the $130 support level, so I adopt the "buy" idea.

data.title: BCE: Hybrid Bonds With 7% Coupon Offer A Solid Income Choice
data.description:
On our previous coverage of BCE Inc. (NYSE:BCE), we went over why we had a 35X larger position in the preferred shares, BCE Inc. PFD 1ST CL AF (TSX:BCE.PR.F:CA). We also highlighted the inevitability of a dividend cut, regardless of what management said.

We think 50% has the best feel here, and a reset to a $2.00 annual dividend (slightly less than exactly 50%) is what we would prepare for. At the high end, BCE could go for $2.40, but why bother? The last thing you want to do is recruit the income investors who don't understand the repair work that needs to be done here.

Source: BCE Tees Up The Big Dividend Cut And Stops Digging That Hole.

BCE cut on schedule and framed the cut as follows.

Balanced capital allocation strategy:

BCE annualized common share dividend established at $1.75 to support deleveraging efforts while providing enhanced flexibility;

net debt leverage ratio of approximately 3.5 times adjusted EBITDA expected by the end of 2027;

discounted treasury issuances terminated under dividend reinvestment plan.

Source: BCE Q1-2025 Press Release.

We will go over the key set of numbers from Q1-2025 and the major deal that adds heft to the dividend cut.

Q1-2025

The overall results for Q1-2025 were on the weak side, and the only reason they look respectable compared to estimates is because estimates were slashed since the beginning of the year.

Revenues were down, and product revenues were down quite significantly. BCE was able to protect and expand its EBITDA margins, though, and its cost-cutting exercise paid off. Capex was lower as well, and that helped expand the free cash flow delta year over year.

At the segment level, BCE's results were particularly weak on postpaid mobile phone subscribers, where it actually saw a loss of about 9,600 customers. Average revenue per subscriber was down 1.8%. Some of this was offset on the Internet and business revenue side, but BCE is facing a tough macro environment, and it shows.

Bell Media, on the other hand, looked a bit better. Revenues were up, and adjusted EBITDA was up a lot. The results were skewed by Outfront Media Inc's (OUT) Canadian asset acquisition, which was completed in Q2-2024.

The good news for investors was that BCE maintained its financial guidance for the year. As bad as these numbers look, they could be worse.

BCE also threw in a new deal, which will decrease the capex on Ziply assets.

BCE, through Ziply, will retain a 49% equity stake in the partnership with PSP owning 51%. PSP has been an investor in Ziply and knows the management team well. To be clear, and this is important, BCE will own 100% of Ziply's existing operations, subscribers, and financials.

Ziply, as a BCE subsidiary, will continue its Fiber expansion within its remaining copper footprint. Ziply will also retain all retail customer relationships associated with the incremental Fiber locations to be built by the Strategic Partnership. What the partnership will be focused on is building the last-mile Fiber in Ziply's growth markets.

This includes the near-term development of approximately one million Fiber passings in Ziply's existing states, with the ability to expand to six million Fiber locations longer term, this will enable Ziply to eventually reach up to eight million total Fiber locations, an increase from its original target of three million.

The strategic partnership structure is a cost-effective and capital-efficient way to fund our U.S.

Source: BCE Q1-2025 Conference Call Transcript.

This, alongside the dividend cut, should accelerate the deleveraging process.

Outlook & Verdict

BCE gives you a 12% free cash flow yield and a 5.5% dividend yield. The valuation is not too demanding on an EV to EBITDA basis, and BCE is trading close to 6.4X our estimates. Now, it is important to note where the major competitors are trading, and TELUS Corporation (TU) certainly looks extremely expensive here.

But BCE presents a longer-term value and one where we can see material upside if they get debt under 3.0X. Asset sales and retained cash flow (the difference between free cash flow yield and dividend yield) should help them. But it is going to be a long slog. Getting under 3.0X is a 5-year plan.

Can you make good returns here? Sure. Imagine a 2% EBITDA growth per year and BCE making it to its 2030 target. Then add on a 2-multiple expansion on EV to EBITDA. This might not seem like a major move for the stock, but it actually resulted in a doubling of the stock price. Factor in the 27.5% returns from dividends, and you are talking about some big bucks. We think that is actually a reasonable path for BCE, though we might see a few more rocky quarters. The biggest headwind is the reduced immigration policy from Canada. Fewer bodies mean fewer cell phones, and in this environment, it makes BCE's job that much harder.

For our part, we have, as before, a small position in the common shares. The preferreds were our weapon of choice, and they remain so today.

For U.S. Investors

Until this point, we had written the article from the lens of the Canadian investor, and all references were in Canadian Dollars. We are going to add a little information here on the Hybrid/Subordinated bonds issued recently in U.S. Dollars, which are equivalent to the preferred shares trading on TSX. Investors should keep in mind that they are subordinated and not the same as the regular bonds of BCE. We put out the following trade alert for this in early April within our Investing Groups' Service, Conservative Income Portfolio.

BCE CORP SUBORDINATED BONDS. CUSIP 0778FPAQ2. These have a coupon of 7%. Mature on Sep 15 , 2055. Can be called on Sep 15, 2035. If not called, they reset at new yield, which is the higher of 7% or 5 Year US Treasury plus 2.363%. We are buying $20,000 par value at $99.4251.

Source: Conservative Income Portfolio.

That is a fantastic deal, as it protects you from higher and lower interest rates. The 7% minimum would kick in if US 5-year treasury (US5Y) notes went below 4.637% in 2025. The spread function would kick in if rates were higher. Of course, BCE can call this, but this is about the best bond with almost no interest rate risk. It only has credit risk of BCE. These are currently trading at $100.40 and remain a good deal for U.S. investors and Canadians with US dollars to invest.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult with a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

data.title: AMD Q1: You Still Have Time To Buy Before The Sentiment Shift
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD), the stock has gained 5% in price.

AMD reported excellent Q1 2025 results, with 36% year-over-year growth from revenues of $7.44 billion. The standout in the quarter, though, was the Data Center segment, with robust demand for EPYC server processors and Instinct GPUs. Growth such as this is not surprising given the tailwinds from the emerging AI revolution.

Non-GAAP earnings rose 55% to $1.57 billion. This is evidence of good operational leverage with scaling revenues. Despite all of this momentum, there are certainly significant near-term headwinds for AMD. Most prominently, U.S. export controls, which management estimates may negatively impact 2025 revenues by approximately $1.5 billion.

However, as an optimistic assumption, a 12-month price target of $200 does not seem impossible to me. This would result from profit margin expansion, additional market share gains, and macro tailwinds, tempered by China pressures.

Q1 Analysis

AMD's Q1 performance was led by the Data Center segment, with spectacular year-over-year revenues of 57% to around $3.67 billion. Alliances with industry giants, like Meta (META) and Google (GOOGL) (GOOG), affirm AMD's increasing role in providing infrastructure for the AI revolution. The Client segment also improved, rising year-over-year by 68% to $2.3 billion. This was due to high demand for the new Zen 5 Ryzen processors. However, the Gaming segment contracted, with revenues declining year-over-year by 30% due to softening semi-custom demand as the console generation matures.

Gross margins rose to 50% on a GAAP basis, from 47% a year ago. This reflects data center products with higher margins. Operating margins expanded to 24% on a non-GAAP basis, reflecting cost discipline and scale in operations.

There was a relatively modest year-over-year revenue decline of just 3%, to $823 million, for AMD's Embedded segment. However, this segment still provides diversification and stable margins.

AMD's acquisition of ZT Systems further contributes to AMD's vertical integration, enabling the company to more effectively monetize a meaningful share of its TAM in AI infrastructure products; the market could be $500 billion in size by 2028, according to some current dominant estimates. I think it will be higher as macroeconomic conditions improve and the progress of AI becomes much more obvious socio-operationally.

Despite its strengths, the company does face headwinds from regulatory risks. Most notably, export controls on advanced AI chips. The company has already outlined an $800 million inventory charge for Q2 2025.

Valuation & Macro Analysis

AMD stock could reach $200 in a bull-case outcome in 12 months' time. However, more conservatively, we are looking at about $175 in a base case. In this analysis, I will run you through the bull-case outcome. I am growing more optimistic about positive macroeconomic outcomes, which could cause very strong stock market sentiment amid strong growth in 2026.

I anticipate AMD achieving a TTM non-GAAP net margin of about 30% in mid-2026, driven by continued favorable product mix shifts and operational leverage from increased data center revenue. With 1.65 billion shares outstanding and projected TTM revenue of about $37 billion in mid-2026, AMD would generate approximately $11 billion in non-GAAP net income and non-GAAP EPS of $6.75.

Applying a non-GAAP P/E multiple of 30 is justified by the company's robust revenue growth, expanding margins, and strategic position within the high-growth AI and data center markets. This results in a 12-month price target of $200, as specified earlier.

Technically speaking, we are still experiencing downward pressure on the stock price. However, to get the best returns in the market, you have to pre-empt the inflection point. I believe it has already happened, but you haven't missed the major bull run that's coming if you buy in now. If you look closely at the following chart, you can see the start of recovery in April. This upward momentum has been building for about a month now. I find it very unlikely that further downward pressure will resume given favorable macro indicators, like the major U.S.-UK trade deal setting the stage for further international deals to come. These deals will remove non-tariff barriers and set the stage for a bull market in 2026 based on deregulation and tax tailwinds, as well as a reformed free-trade horizon. I wrote about this recently in an Editor's Pick I achieved on Seeking Alpha, and it is nice to see my thesis coming to fruition.

Risk Analysis

I consider AMD to be a low-risk bet on AI infrastructure, but several risks could impact my price target. Regulatory and geopolitical challenges remain prominent regarding U.S. semiconductor export controls. The $800 million inventory write-down expected in Q2 (due to export restrictions to China for advanced AI chips) underscores the materiality of this risk. That said, the Trump administration may potentially ease these export restrictions, which were implemented under Biden. The Biden-era "AI Diffusion Rule" will be revoked, but I expect that as AMD and Nvidia (NVDA) keep pressing the administration for continued global chip influence, China will be allowed to trade with the U.S. under careful U.S. oversight.

There is also the overbearing risk of AMD's reliance on TSMC (TSM). It will probably be a few years before TSMC is operational at scale in Arizona, so for the time being, the risk of a Chinese invasion of Taiwan looms. The quicker the newly outlined TSMC Arizona plants are active, the better. I'm still holding about 17.5-20% in cash due to the Taiwan invasion risk. I'd like to deploy some more of that cash (potentially down to about 12.5-15%), but at the moment it still seems too risky.

The Achilles heel of Western capitalism really is Taiwan right now. We should just be thankful that a Chinese invasion would be the equivalent of a bee sting: China would self-destruct its long-term reputation and near-term economics on impact, while the U.S.-led world order would most likely survive (with gained bolstered international reputation and the associated long-term geo-economic security).

Conclusion: Strong Buy

AMD's robust Q1 2025 performance clarifies its position in the emerging AI revolution. Despite China-related regulatory headwinds facing AMD, value-generating deals and relentless innovation still put it squarely in secular growth markets. With realistic macroeconomic assumptions and sensible multiples, my model comes out with an enticing 12-month target of $200 per share in a bull-case outcome. This valuation reflects anticipated margin improvements, ongoing market share gains, and macroeconomic stability.

That said, investors must carefully monitor key risks, particularly geopolitical developments and competitive dynamics. I'm an AMD shareholder, and I hold it at close to 10% of my portfolio right now. I believe it will be one of the most rewarding and least-risky stocks to capitalize on strong AI market sentiment in 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Palantir: It's Just Too Expensive Here (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I have been bullish on Palantir Technologies Inc.'s (NASDAQ:PLTR) stock for several years now. In fact, I built this badly beaten-down stock and highly misunderstood company into my most significant holding in late 2022 and early 2023, when its share price was only around $7.

Palantir - Double Top Pattern

To say that Palantir has done well is a massive understatement. This stock has been on fire, appreciating by a mind-bending 2,000% (peak-to-trough) in only about two years. Palantir was also the best performing stock in in the S&P 500 (SP500) in 2024.

Of course, I decreased my Palantir position along the way. What was once a 10-15% portfolio holding is now only about 2% despite the enormous price appreciation.

Now, I still love the company because it's dominant in its field and should continue to be very successful. Nonetheless, the primary reason why I decreased much of my Palantir position around the $80-100 level was because the valuation became hyper-elevated.

This dynamic made the prospects of making more considerable near-term highs very unlikely. Also, technically, the stock had become staggeringly overbought above $100 in early February.

We witnessed a massive "correction" leading up into and after April 2. The stock dropped from about $125 to approximately $65, roughly a 48% decline in only about five weeks.

The Report Was Good, But Not Out Of This World Good

Palantir reported earnings recently, and while the results were solid, they were not stellar, in my view. Yes, most of the key metrics were better than expected, and the guidance was strong, but we did not witness a "Nvidia Moment."

Palantir reported Q1 EPS of 13 cents (Non-GAAP), which was inline with estimates. The company also provided 8 cents in GAAP EPS, beating the consensus estimate by one penny. Revenue came in at about $884M, nearly a 40% YoY increase.

While the sales guidance was higher, it was about 4% above the consensus estimates, which is not 40%. Also, some analysts are expressing international growth concerns following the quarter. I would add that the stock's valuation has swelled so obscenely that it may need more significant growth to justify further increases.

Let's Remain Rational

Palantir now has a market cap of about $280 billion. I remember when $100 billion seemed insane. Why? Because Palantir had only about $2.87 billion in sales last year, and could achieve about $3.9B in sales this year, and roughly $5B in revenues next year.

So, we are dealing with a stock that trades close to 100 times TTM sales, and even if we value Palantir relative to next year's sales projections, its valuation is around 55-60 times 2026 expected sales.

This valuation is uber-expensive, and if we look at Palantir from a P/E basis, it's still extremely costly here.

Palantir could earn about $0.58 this year, and roughly $0.73 next year. This dynamic illustrates that Palantir's forward P/E ratio is over 200, and its 2026 P/E ratio is around 160. In a more bullish case outcome, I believe Palantir could deliver about $0.80 in EPS next year.

Yet, this still puts Palantir's forward P/E ratio at about 150, extremely elevated, and likely limiting near term upside potential in Palantir's stock. This dynamic also makes the stock more vulnerable to a considerable correction as the ultra high valuation could lead to increased selling pressure as we advance.

When To Buy The Dip In Palantir

There are some clear dip buying opportunities in Palantir, but they must be substantial dips, in my view. We witnessed a solid opportunity on April 7th, as the swift drop to $65 created a very oversold environment for Palantir.

Unfortunately, Palantir is the poster child for a super high multiple stock. Therefore, we may see a more considerable pullback process with Palantir in the near and potentially in the intermediate term, as the market digests Palantir's valuation.

PLTR 1-Year Chart

In a bullish case scenario, Palantir could have only a mild pullback to about $100-90/50-day MA support. However, this pullback would not reset its valuation by much, and there may be limited catalysts to push this stock notably above $100 in the near term.

Therefore, Palantir could potentially decline to its previous support around $65-70, which may coincide with its 200-day MA. However, in a "worst-case" scenario in which the market may want to revalue Palantir more aggressively, we could potentially witness a slide down to the critical $50-40 level support.

I know, it's a long way down, but I've seen much stranger things occur. Therefore, and despite its remarkable potential and dominant market-leading position, it would not surprise me to see Palantir decline to about $50 in the next general market pullback.

Also, at about 50 bucks per share, Palantir would trade at a much more attractive 75 forward (2026) P/E multiple. While it's not cheap, it's better than the current 160 forward P/E.

Also, Palantir could outperform EPS estimates. Therefore, we may see EPS coming in at $0.80-0.90 next year. Now, if Palantir's stock closed the long-standing gap around $40, its forward P/E would be about 45-50 here, which seems like a highly reasonable buy-in valuation for Palantir.

Even around $50, Palantir's valuation of 50-60 on a forward P/E basis becomes very attractive in my view. Palantir could increase EPS YoY from about $0.60-0.80 (33%) in 2026.

Given that its forward P/E could be about 55 at $50, Palantir's PEG ratio would be a much more reasonable 1.66. Currently, Palantir's forward (2026) PEG ratio is at about 4.5, which is very high, and is another reason for its current hold rating.

The Silver Lining

Palantir's stock could continue doing well in the intermediate and long term. I don't want to be negative on Palantir. It's an excellent American company, and it has a remarkable business with enormous potential. Nonetheless, there is such a thing as being too expensive, and the market may need to reevaluate Palantir in the near-term. Still, Palantir has considerable potential in the intermediate and long-term.

Where Palantir's stock could go in future years

Year 2025 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
Revenue (in billions) $3.9 $5.25 $6.8 $8.6 $10.9 $13.6
Revenue growth 34% 35% 29% 27% 26% 25%
EPS $0.61 $0.85 $1.14 $1.48 $1.90 $2.40
EPS growth 49% 39% 34% 30% 29% 25%
Forward P/E 117 105 95 90 80 75
Stock price $100 $120 $140 $170 $192 $205

Source: The Financial Prophet.

I've reduced some of my price and valuation targets since my previous Palantir analysis. Still, I believe Palantir could achieve better than anticipated results in a more bullish case outcome. This dynamic could enable Palantir's stock to appreciate more considerably and more rapidly than anticipated. Nonetheless, we would need to see more considerable growth developments at Palantir, in my view. For now, I've reduced my rating from a buy to a hold and I've adjusted my 2025 price target from $120 to $100.

data.title: One Ring To Rule Them All: The Price Of Oil
data.description:
The relatively rapid decline in oil prices during the past the last months is leading to a different outlook for U.S. oil and gas. Globally, to 2024, we had been producing 22% of the crude and liquids market and 26% of natural gas. That’s a huge supply-side accomplishment.

The pricing impact of shale has been enormous. The Dallas Fed found that oil prices in 2018 would have been around 36% higher if the shale revolution did not take place. Further, the shale revolution implies a reduction in current oil price volatility around 25% and a decline in long-run volatility of more than 50%. Kiss that goodbye. Considering that the price of oil dictates so much around the world, and geopolitics are a key source of volatility, the world may look different.

While the industry has faced such macroeconomic and geopolitical headwinds with aplomb in the past, the geological and economic issues surrounding peak oil are different. I’ll attempt a brief analysis that shows why it’s important. Price discovery of other goods is tied to the price of oil, too.

This research note will analyze the statements recently made by CEO Vicki Hollub of Occidental Petroleum (OXY) and Diamondback (FANG) CEO Travis Stice. I will also corroborate it with other insights and analysis. Hollub says U.S. shale may peak in 2027, but moved it up to 2026 potentially, owing to the volatile macro environment. The spot price of WTI was $73 Jan. 31 and $60 May 2, lowering price to a new marker.

Tipping points

Diamondback CEO Travis Stice said:

"We believe we are at a tipping point for U.S. oil production at current commodity prices. (The) U.S. frac crew count is already down ~15% this year, with the Permian Basin crew count down ~20% from its January peak, and expected to decline further. U.S. oil directed rig counts (are) to be down almost 10% by the end of the second quarter and decline further in the third quarter. (Thus) it is likely that U.S. onshore oil production has peaked and will begin to decline this quarter."

From the shareholder letter of May 5, Stice continues:

“Over the past decade, the cost of supply for the average barrel of oil produced in the United States has increased. The shale revolution has evolved from proof of concept (outspend cash flow to prove up basins) to manufacturing mode (significant growth) and is now in a more mature stage of development (free cash flow generation and return of capital).”

He's acknowledging the recent tariff policy moves that have reduced the price of crude and are now coming home to roost. OPEC, mainly through Saudi Arabia’s leadership, will find its advantage, attempting to reduce shale’s once-growing market share. This call on OPEC was thought to be happening in the 2030s from forecasts five years back, pre-pandemic.

"Geologic headwinds outweighing tailwinds" provided by improvements in technology and operational efficiency were also highlighted by Stice. In a feature of mine from last year, this same idea is presented slightly differently with a similar meaning. Noted by a shale pioneer just one year ago, "At some point the pace of tech advances may slow, that is, the laws of diminishing returns." Last year, global peak oil projections ranged from 106 m/b/d to 116 per IEA and OPEC, respectively. It was thought to go higher yet by transforming globally abundant gas to liquids even. U.S. production was hovering around 13 m/b/d, and 14 million could be seen, “but 15-16 is a stretch,” according to the pioneering executive.

Merge and move forward

More M&A activity might result because of the pricing environment. Consolidations have occurred as long as the industry's been around, notes the executive. Asked about this time, he says, “I think it's different in degrees, though not completely. Consolidations are typically the product of the majors or large companies using one of their big strategic advantages, which is capital.” But the rebuilding of inventory as before, in the wave that set off the first shale revolutions in the 2000-2010s when the independents acquired acreage left behind by the majors, may be different.

There is an emphasis on the next wave of opportunities likely being international, he notes, where shale tech and advancements can be applied for market needs. We don't know what the technology is going to be tomorrow that will create another opportunity,” the pioneer offers. I've seen M&A cycle through with an important effect on the structure of the industry.” For example, large amounts of capital were required to achieve scale in U.S. unconventional resource development. Source: "The Long Runway for New Energy Demand"

The idea "hydrocarbons are still a growth business" from last year is still true, to degrees now, for those that can leverage advantages. They exist. In 2018, BP bought into the Permian:

“This (acquisition) transforms our Lower 48 business and reinforces our position as a top onshore producer in the United States. It adds assets which today produce around 190,000 boe/p/d across 470,000 acres, with around 4.6 billion barrels of discovered resource.”

This was a $10.5 billion purchase of BHP’s assets in Texas, in fact from early successes in the Eagle Ford shale. Today, the $73 billion-cap BP is being consider for acquisition by Shell ( SHEL), Chevron ( CVX), Exxon Mobil ( XOM), TotalEnergies ( TTE) and Abu Dhabi's Adnoc as they analyze the potential, says Seeking Alpha from sources.

M&A was slowing from the big wave occurring from 2021 to 2024. But now, more is evident in the oil and gas patch. At a February NAPE conference in Houston, a top private equity leader noted other successes regarding shale’s overall economic impact:

  • WTI prices: 24% decrease ($100 to $76 per barrel)
  • Natural gas: 73% decrease
  • Utility gas: 2% decrease

On peaking and impact

If U.S. shale peaks in the 13 million b/d range, and declines, associated gas production also declines. Associated gas comprises roughly 25-30% of produced gas. The peak of U.S. oil was predicted in the 2027-2030 range by Hollub at a conference just two weeks ago. This shows you the effect of the deteriorated environment that executives now have to navigate, especially around first quarter earnings.

Further, I parsed research on peaking of resources in 2010, with the following takeaways that are relevant now:

“Any well-functioning market economy, endowed with a limited amount of an exhaustible resource, will apportion production through time according to prevailing economic incentives that reflect market fundamentals, by which I mean the cost of production, discount rates, the strength of current versus future demand, and the availability of substitutes.” —James Smith, energy economist, formerly of SMU Cox School of Business.

In a market-driven economy, this is how it works. “Producers are driven by attempts to maximize profits,” Smith explains. “As a producer, I have oil reserves. If I believe they will be really scarce in five years I’m not going to produce as much today because I’ll be rewarded more later when price is higher. So I leave these resources in the ground. That’s part of an inter-temporal balancing.”

This is exactly what is happening. What does it mean? Production will slow as the large gas players had to do in 2023-2024, owing to low prices, and now with oil-weighted firms doing the same. Oil prices will ultimately rise to meet the economics of production and shareholder expectations of free cash flow-induced dividends. I’ve seen modeling of what types of acreage are drilled at $90 WTI versus $70. Lower oil prices dictate less drilling of remaining core acreages in key basins.

Additionally, economics will also rule. Sticky prices and production, ie., supply and demand inelasticities, will reveal the true prices we are to pay. Stagflation could become visible. The charts are changing.

The price of oil is a vital economic proxy for the health of the global economic order, the one price to rule them all. This has been a driver of the last decades. Interesting is what comes next. The oil patch has invariably received a shock, from a more moderate trajectory of the past. The very volatility that shale helped curtail in oil markets is back.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: This Time Is Different
data.description:
"Recession Warnings Are Everywhere, Except in the Data."

So read the headlines of the article by Ben Casselman and Colby Smith in the New York Times.

"Evidence for the economic impact of President Trump's trade wars is everywhere--except, for the most part, in economic data itself."

"Consumer spending hasn't fallen. Layoffs haven't risen. Businesses haven't stopped investing in equipment or buying supplies.

"Economists say it is a matter of time before the impact of tariffs and the uncertainty that Trump's on-again, off-again approach to trade policy has created begin to show up in the hard data."

"But, until then, they are left sifting through crumbs of evidence that wouldn't get a second glance in more normal times: customs revenue, hotel bookings in Las Vegas, freight shipments by truck and rail."

"The problem is we don't have much to hang onto at this point," said Marc Giannoni, chief U.S. economist for Barclays.

"We have to rely on anecdotes, on indicators that are nonconventional."

This time is different!

One should smile after this last sentence.

However, I would argue that this time is different.

I would argue that investors need to keep their eyes on the banking system.

I would argue that the economy has lots and lots of money "hanging around." Money that can be drawn on and used to support "normal" spending patterns.

The money is there...the economic system just needs to draw on it.

In fact, I have been writing about the presence of these monies in the commercial banking system for a long time.

My question is...is this the time that these monies are going to be used? Is this the time that the "excess reserves" in the banking system will be drawn on?

Is this time different?

Let's look at the commercial banking system.

These "Reserve Balances" with Federal Reserve Banks represent the cash balances that commercial banks hold either within their own vaults, with other banks, and with the Federal Reserve.

Notice: something happened in 2008.

Here we find that Ben Bernanke was at work. As I have discussed in many different times and in many different places, Mr. Bernanke, as Chairman of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System, changed how the Federal Reserve functioned when it came to the conduct of monetary policy.

Many changes took place within the commercial banking system, the financial markets, and the Federal Reserve System at this time. We are living with the consequences of these changes.

From 2008 through to 2020, the banking system lived through three rounds of quantitative easing, a time when the Fed increased its holdings of government securities in a regularly, pre-planned approach. As can be seen, the "Reserve Balances" or excess reserves in the commercial banking system increased as this quantitative easing took place.

Reserve Balances before 2008 varied in a range from $5 billion to $35 billion.

Reserve Balances first hit $1 trillion around 2010 and remained above this total since then.

In 2020, the Covid-19 pandemic hit and the Federal Reserve moved into "fast gear" in order to protect the American financial system from collapsing.

By 2020, reserve balancers exceeded $4 trillion.

In March 2020, the Federal Reserve began to reduce the size of its securities portfolio, going into a mode of quantitative tightening to reverse the injection of money into the commercial banking system that it had been doing before.

Since March 2020, the Federal Reserve has continued to engage in quantitative tightening, to where reserve balances now stand at more than $3.2 trillion in the most recent Federal Reserve statistics.

This number is very close to the line item "Cash Assets" found on the Federal Reserve statistical release H.8, "Assets and Liabilities of Commercial Banks in the United States." In the banking week ending April 23, commercial banks in the U.S. held around $3.6 trillion in "Cash Assets."

The point I have been trying to make in my recent writing on this topic is that commercial banks, in spite of all the rules and regulations that work around the cash holdings of these organizations, the commercial banking system has a lot of excess reserves hanging around.

The commercial banking system is in good shape and has the "cash" on hand to support a fairly substantial "burst" in liquidity in the financial system if the right situation arises.

That is, the Federal Reserve would not have to go through a period where it generated a lot of bank stimulus to get the banking system lending, to keep the economy spending, and to avoid a recession at this time.

This is why "Consumer spending hasn't fallen. Layoffs haven't risen. Businesses haven't stopped investing in equipment or buying supplies."

We don't have sufficient proof yet to really say that this is really what is happening.

But, it seems to me that it could be an adequate argument for the result.

If so, it would make "this time different!"

So, I will argue that investors should watch the evolution taking place in the economy and consider this argument as a reason for what we are seeing.

As Mr. Giannoni suggested, as cited above, "The problem is we don't have much to hang onto at this point."

We must look elsewhere in order to understand.

But, we have never had so much money "pumped" into the economy as we have had "pumped" in to fight Covid-19.

There is no question that the commercial banking system has accumulated a lot of "excess cash."

There is no question that the commercial banking system has, up to this point in time, sat on the cash and not put it out to use.

But, the time may have arrived that the banking system feels OK in expanding the use of these funds.

As a consequence, this time could be different.
